INTRODUCTION Healthcare is one of the most  favorable  fields for   implement ing the  I nternet  o f  T hings  and related technologies such as blockchain.  Given the increasing global population, common lifestyles that lead to health-related problems such as diabetes or obesity, and the inefficiency of current healthcare services that recent COVID-19 pandemics showed, there is a broad space for healthcare field  development ( Tripathi et al., 2020) .  Furthermore, as  healthcare unites a complex structure of multiple health domains,  modern technologies  can significantly enhance its public benefits , for instance,  through  the  introduction of smart devices and analytics. With the development of 5G network, Artificial Intelligence, and global data volumes, the Internet of Things or (IoT) will inevitably be the topic of  huge  interest in the nearest future. As a result, the market for the Internet of Things grows steadily. The number of wearables, mobile devices, and various other gadgets is expected to exceed the number of people on the planet  ( MarketWatch, 2020 ). In  2018 there were approximately 7, 2 billion devices, and the historical statistic shows that this number was multiplying five times faster than the population.   The market value of the Internet of Things was estimated at 194 billion US dollars. By 2025 it is expected to exceed 650 billio n  US dollars showing the compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 21 percent, starting from 2020  (Gartner) . The  positive growth  can be explained by advanced data analytics, cost reductions of connected devices, and increased adoption of cloud platforms. IoT helps to connect various smart devices that can exchange information among each other. There are several examples of smart devices: smartphones, sensors, and wearables that collect information that is, in turn,  analyzed  and used to develop customer journey. IoT helps not only customers and their product and service experience but business too. Global retailers can collect insights on the performance of their products, find new customers, and create modern solutions for customer engagement. IoT can change whole industries,  including healthcare.  Further integration of data analytics  in various fields  will enhance IoT utilization and, thus, uncover IoT week points.  A t the moment , IoT solutions have numerous disadvantages, such as issues connected with the safety of transfers, data circulation, and data  sharing  ( Makhdom  et al., 2019 ) .  The distinctive characteristic of IoT is the personal nature of data that is collected by the devices. For instance, home smart devices can uncover enormous information about individuals' habits, preferences, and sensitive personal information that can be used in a manipulative manner.  The personal nature of information highlights the need for data transfers to be protected.  In IoT devices that would collect information on personal health, data safety becomes even more sound. S tarting from 2017, the governments thought about solutions that would make IoT safer. Due to the set of cybersecurity violations in different countries, governments prepared IoT  safety laws. One of the example s  of the law  is the  “Internet of Things Cybersecurity Improvement Act” introduced to the US Senate.  Even though  this act  lists a set of rules to be followed by IoT actors to enhance safety, the act does  not solve the sa fety of IoT but rather poses barriers to its widespread use . Nevertheless ,  several  issues  connected with the safety of data transfers and efficient data sharing  can be  potentially  solved by implementing blockc hain technology. The b lockchain market is expected to grow from $3 billion  in 2020 to $40 billion  in 2026.   Blockchain helps devices to exchange smart contracts in a secure distributed environment. To help adopt blockchain in IoT, a special organization, "Trusted IoT Alliance," was launched. What is more,  several  cryptocurrencies were developed based on IoT . Even though the success of these currencies is limited, they are still worth mentioning.  These currencies are , for instance : IoT Coin, IOTA , and  IoT Chain.   However, a limited number of blockchain solutions consider implementation in the  h ealthcare sector  as it is highly regulated .  Nevertheless, the immutability, transparency, security, and accountability that blockchain can provide can potentially solve sensible healthcare data that limits the introduction of IoT in the healthcare  industry. Moreover, blockchain  can create  trustless  and powerful healthcare service s  – a smart healthcare ecosystem (SHE). Even though there is a growing interest in the use of blockchain in the Internet of Things, the number of existing  articles  on the topic of “Blockchain in IoT” investigate and describe in detail  either  technical sides of potential blockchain   implementation  in IoT or theoretical benefits blockchain-enabled IoT . Meanwhile, limited articles mention the  actual current effect of b lockchain o n the I o T industry . Moreover,  while concentrating on the theoretical concepts and technical characteristics of both blockchain and IoT,  existing  research paper s  do   not cover the  business  potential  of blockchain  implementation and its value  to  customers. Therefore, the purpose of the paper is to cover the gap and add to the scientific field  theoretical knowledge on the feasibility of blockchain-enabled IoT creation and a   survey -based investigation of the consumers’ perceptions towards b lockchain -enabled  IoT  devices in healthcare. The research question is:  What potentia l does blockchain technology have  for  its  integration with IoT in the healthcare industry  from a consumer and business perspective ?  The word  “ potential ”  is defined from 2 distinct perspectives: 1) potential as the  feasibility   of current blockchain business  solutions f or  its integration in IoT , and 2) potential as  a level of  technology  acceptance from the customers. The subsequent questions that will help to answer the main  research question  are: What challenges does IoT in healthcare face? What impact can blockchain adoption have on IoT and healthcare? What is the potential of blockchain implementation  in healthcare IoT? To answer the research question, the following tasks are to be completed: Understanding of technical characteristics of blockchain and IoT  essential for the healthcare industry  through the literature  overview; Identification of the key   blockchain characteristics valuable for IoT  development; Exploration of the technology acceptance  models; Examination of the current IoT and blockchain projects in  healthcare; Creation of a set of hypotheses  on the consumers’ perceptions relevant for blockchain-based IoT in  healthcare;   Collection of data  from consumers , including blockchain  consumers ; Testing the set  of  hypotheses; Statistical analysis of the data by SEM and g eneralization of  results; Provision of  the summary on the current development of the field (blockchain-enabled IoT in healthcare ) ; Formulation of  recommendatio ns aimed at enhancing the practical application of b lockchain in  healthcare IoT from a business and consumer perspective. The paper aims to describe the existing potential o f blockchain implementation to h ealthcare IoT devices from a business and consumer perspective.  The results will be of value for academicians who research the topics of "blockchain," "IoT," and "healthcare" as a theoretical foundation for further research. Moreover, the paper will summarize the real-life perceptions of the general audiences towards blockchain and IoT and present the recent state of blockchain  solutions  for IoT  from a  business feasibility perspective. Therefore, the results can be used by the actors in the healthcare field, clinics directors, and governmental representatives to enhance the positive perception of the discussed technologies to enable their implementation. In addition, the paper can be useful for business entities that would like to implement blockchain-enabled solutions for IoT in healthcare to understand the recent state of the business in the field. The structure of the paper starts with the  inand  Literature overview, which discusses the blockchain and IoT characteristics essential for the healthcare  industry and the potential for the development of the technology . Further, the methodology is described for both 1) identifying the potential  of blockchain-enabled IoT for healthcare  from a business perspective and 2) from a consumer perspective. Then, the data analysis part  presents the result of the content analysis of current solutions in the market and  summarizes the key statistical operations conducted with the data collected  from consumers. Furthermore, the paper  presents  key  results  followed by the ir  Discussion  and provision of the summary of the potential of blockchain-enabled IoT for the healthcare industry . Finally, a conclusion with theoretical and practical contributions and further research directions is presented. LITERATURE REVIEW A literature review is structured around three main theoretical concepts: IoT, Blockchain, and Healthcare, and investigates the synergies between these terms. The  review aims to identify and describe existing IoT weaknesses and Blockchain characteristics that can overcome the IoT weaknesses.  Moreover, the review specifies crucial features of both blockchain and IoT needed for their implementation in the Healthcare sector  and discussed the potential of implementing the blockchain-based IoT in Healthcare. C hapter  1. Internet of things in the healthcare industry.   I nternet of Things The concept of IoT was first applied in 1982, where an Internet-connected vending machine could send feedback on the temperature of drinks it contained.  IoT's widespread recognition after  Walmart combined RFID tags to pallets to better monitor its supply chain in 2005. The subsequently obtained product information could then be shared with suppliers. In the years after, IoT became an important strategic asset in business' plans. IoT was adopted in all industries, with research on self-driving vehicles, smart home appliances, IoT in healthcare, smart glasses, etc. ( Khvoynitskaya , 2019). Increasingly more companies nowadays install IoT-related hardware into their products. With its monitoring, controlling, checking, and  analyzing  functionalities, IoT has become a pivotal asset to a wide variety of companies in every industry with the potential to be disruptive ( Lueth , 2014).  At its core, IoT technology can be characterized by the data streams between interconnected devices. These devices contain sensors that obtain data according to the device’s usage. Data is then shared with the operator of a particular IoT platform and stored in a cloud. Platform operators collect, process, and  analyze  the data before integrating it into the software of IoT devices. According to analyses and unveiled patterns in the aggregated data, an IoT platform provider can get a clear view of the needs of its users and incorporate this into a personalized user experience (Xia et al. 2012).  Different researchers are describing  ioT . Mistry et al. (2019) writes: "IoT is the network of various distinct electronic or electrical devices capable of interacting with each other using any open channel such as the Internet". Internet of Things is also described as an  "omnipresence of a variety of things, where they can interact and cooperate to provide a wide range of services" ( Tahar  and  Badis   Hammi ,  Bellot ,  Serhrouchni , 2018). In 2008, the number of connected devices exceeded the world population, and, as of 2020, there are around 50 billion connected devices ( Khvoynitskaya , 2019). IoT is one of the key elements of the Fourth Industrial Revolution, where the functionality of devices exceeds the functionality of human  labor  in a lot of practices (Xu et al., 2018). IoTs flourishing interest worldwide was pushed by its addition to the Gartner Hype Cycle for emerging technologies in 2011. After reaching inflated expectations in the late 2010s, the plateau is estimated to be reached between 2025 and 2030 ( Khvoynitskaya , 2019).  IoT is widely used from daily life applications - like smart home devices and wearables - to applications in 'smart cities (i.e., traffic distribution, safety, waste) to appliances in cars and healthcare ( Macaronne , 2020). 1.1.1. Key characteristics of IoT   Wireless sensor networks such as Bluetooth, RFID, WSN or  WiFi  enable the transformation of technologies into smart technologies that can communicate with each other.  Minoli  et al (2018) created an IoT framework that consists of 7 layers: Things (Sensors), Data Acquisition (Devices), Fog Networking (LAN, BAN), Data Aggregation (Edge Gateway), Data Centralization (Public cloud), Data analytics and storage (Internet and apps storages), and Applications.  Not only there are different kinds of IoT devices, IoT can be applied to different spheres of business around the world, which are Automobile industry, segment of Utilities, Building automation, physical security, Manufacturing and natural resources, Healthcare providers, Retail and wholesale trade, Information, Transportation,  Healthcare  and others. Its application grows continuously.  IoT is a set of interrelated and interconnected devices that share data among each other every 5-10 seconds without human interaction . Via the internet, the Internet of Things can connect multiple devices that are embedded in different systems. Digitally represented by devices, these systems can be accessed by anyone, anywhere. The communication or connectivity between devices then allows for the collection of significant amounts of various data from different devices in different places around the globe, which provides more opportunities for improving the efficiency and security of the IoT. The IoT is a revolutionary force that enables organizations to improve their operational efficiency for the best results by  analyzing  and securing the Internet of  Things.   Therefore,  it is possible to discuss IoT as a technological ecosystem that needs to be secure,  safe  and reliable  to enable its growth and the development of numerous industries . One of the most important characteristics of the Internet of Things is  the  sensitivity of personal information collected by smart devices.  That is why various layers, such as Data Acquisition, Data Aggregation, Centralization, Analysis and Storage are being  transform  to solve the privacy issue.   For example, fog networking Layer with its BAN protocol introduced exactly to make personal data collected from Things less exposed to threat.  One can understand the importance of data safety by imagining a person driving an automobile, connected to the network through which all vehicles and drivers ’  movement s  can be seen in real-time. Another good example is a cardio medical device that collect s  information on specific characteristics of the body. This data is enormously sensitive and can lead to irreparable consequences, if being manipulated. IoT is a developing concept that can enable multiple industries with its data connectivity characteristic. However, the sensitivity of IoT collects data is of the concern that is yet to be solved. 1.1. 2 . Limitations of current IoT devices Meanwhile,  Makhdom  et al (2019) lists IoT devices’ vulnerabilities such as lack of physical security, the absence of host-based defence mechanisms (e.g., anti-virus), lack of software updates and security patches, lack of access control measures, and cross-device dependencies. All  the  drawbacks exist due to hallmarks of the existing IoT devices, such as low memory, limited abilities to compute tasks, and small batteries that cannot live long.  Owing to the small memory and battery life, it is impossible to conduct  complex computational tasks, but it also limits the access control measures. Moreover, due to the IoT as ecosystem and cross-device dependencies, the whole system is possibly corrupted if one of the inter-connected devices is corrupted. Overall, t he problem that arises due to the sensitivity of information that IoT devices collect is sound as most IoT devices lack substantial security patches and defence mechanisms.  It is expected that when problems of security and IoT hallmarks are solved, the IoT market will be developing at an even faster pace. 1.2.   I o T in Healthcare Tripathi et al. (2020) believe that healthcare is one of the most desirable fields for implementing IoT and other smart technologies based on big data.  The COVID-19 pandemic has brought the healthcare system across the world under scrutiny. It wouldn't be a revelation to say that there is a lot of scope for improvement and a long way to go for the industry to achieve its purpose. There will be a global reality check for long-pending national healthcare policies and regulatory reforms. The Global Industry Classification Standard and the Industry Classification Benchmark have classified the healthcare industry into two main groups: healthcare equipment, services and pharmaceuticals, and biotechnology-related life science (Market Research Report). The paper focuses on the first categorisation – healthcare services and equipment.  Over 2019 - 2023, health spending is expected to rise at a CAGR of 5 percent, up from 2.7 percent seen in 2014–2018 (Economist intelligence unit, 2019).  With the 2019 global population of 7.7 billion expected to reach 8.5 billion by 2030 (Department of economics and social affair, 2019), meeting healthcare needs will be an overarching challenge for healthcare providers.  Overall life expectancy is projected to increase from an estimated 73.7 years in 2018 to 74.7 years by 2023 (Economics Intelligence Unit, 2019). The number of people aged over 65 will be more than 11.8 percent of the total population (Economics Intelligence Unit, 2019). Lifestyle factors like obesity, smoking, poor diet, hypertension, and lack of physical activity are among the top 10 causes of death. As we have realised from COVID 19, the spread of communicable diseases (CMD) is many-fold due to globalisation. While CMDs continue to pose a threat, non-communicable diseases (NCMD) like diabetes are also rising (Allen, 2019). Although treating diseases is currently given an essential role in the industry, a rising need is for the industry to shift from working towards cure to prevention and early intervention.  The constant innovation and technological development directly impact the formulation of cure and research in the field. However, when we look at the industry's business side, technology has been mainly used to digitise documents, make online appointments, etc. The industry is yet to exploit its potential fully. However, as we speak, the focus is now shifting towards improving patient outcomes and providing a better patient experience, increasing clinician satisfaction, streamlining business processes, implementing innovative practices and programs, improving overall organizational productivity, maximizing revenue, and managing costs ( Tsan , 2018). Introducing technologies and intelligent devices into the healthcare routine can help providers gain essential information about patients' health conditions and provide quality preventive healthcare services. Smart healthcare The healthcare industry today is not a secluded zone, avoided by the wonders of modern technological advances. Thanks to technological advances and the confluence of informatics, electronics, and related technologies, it has become feasible to build an intelligent healthcare environment in which all healthcare entities are connected and can communicate. Current trends in the healthcare market further support this concept. The technologies such as IoT, Artificial intelligence, Machine Learning, Big Data, Cloud computing, and so on, make the S mart Healthcare System (S HS )  a reality. Countries and nations around the globe are starting to realize the potential behind the adoption of modern ways of servicing the medical industry and are successfully working towards the implementation.  Components  of SHS Rapidly developing technologies have allowed the healthcare industry to introduce state of the art technologies such as the Internet of Things, a rtificial intelligence, robotics, etc., to its advantage and reform the traditional ways of functioning.  F ig ure  1  presents the essential elements of SHS that enable them to provide modern diagnostics and treatment tools and make more informed and informed decisions like never-before. Fig ure  1 .  Components  of smart healthcare system  (Tripathi et al., 2020). The objects in the healthcare system (such as monitoring equipment, medical devices and so on) can connect to the network and exchange information among each other through The  Internet of Things and the wireless sensor network. In addition, wearable devices allow for real time monitoring of patients and quick response to any alterations in vital parameters.  The large amounts of data created by these devices can be saved in the cloud and retrieved and  analyzed  using big data analytics techniques to provide detailed information about the patient's state, allowing for more educated and informed decisions about treatment and diagnosis. Historical data may also offer use cases for research and development, and systems can be trained to anticipate illnesses and propose viable therapies and prescriptions using artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques.   This type of predictive healthcare already exists. This information can also help with robotics and other automated systems. As a result, contemporary technologies coexist and constitute an intelligent healthcare system  (Tripathi et al., 2020) . Health monitoring has been fully transformed by introducing wearable IoT sensors that are typically non-invasive tiny devices and can monitor the physiological state and essential health metrics of patients with minimal or no involvement by medical personnel (nurses, ward boys). The Internet of Things-based healthcare system has shown to be indispensable in various situations, including permitting remote monitoring of the elderly for health-related concerns and allowing patients to receive treatment at home rather than in a hospital. IoT technology enables care professionals (doctors, nurses, etc.) to remotely monitor, diagnose, and propose treatments or offer emergency care if needed. Additionally, IoT-based healthcare collects data and information that can be used to provide detailed analysis of the patients' various physiological, cognitive, and physical conditions to provide better healthcare services such as predictive healthcare, non-invasive treatments and clinical trials, round-the-clock monitoring, and so on. A range of applications and framework proposals in literature are created due to the growing relevance of sensors for various objectives. Furthermore, according to “Allied Market Research,” the wearable devices market would develop at an exponential rate by the end of 2022. Several sensors on the market have  particular functionality  and use depending on the kind and nature of the data to be gathered and evaluated. Fig. 1 illustrates a typical wearable sensor's basic components. A sensor is a thumbnail that can sensor the surroundings when there is an event of interest. The sensor data are translated into electrical signals initially. Then these electrical impulses must finally be transmitted to the cloud storage system. That implies connecting the sensors/appliances via a certain media. This media is usually wireless such as Bluetooth, low power broadband, mobile and  Wi F i  networks, etc. The usage of a particular connection media depends now directly on the detecting activity the sensor or gadget carries out  (Tripathi et al., 2020) .  Wearable IoT-based gadgets: Doctors utilize these devices to monitor the patients' status in real-time. They are just like bracelets that you may wear easily on the body. These wearable gadgets include sensors that scan the surroundings and patient vital signs (for example, body temperature, blood pressure, ECG, humidity, etc). If these parameters differ (from the allowable limit), doctors and clinicians will be alerted in real-time. The message comes to the concerned doctors on the smart devices and as an SMS. Clinical data/Electronic health records: The constant patient surveillance produces huge amounts of data. These data are not always helpful in real-time but are important for intelligent analysis. Therefore, all data collected from the intelligent wearable device and other monitoring devices is saved in the cloud. In addition, the blockchain hashing algorithm encodes data before storage so that any data theft is prevented. Encrypted/Decryption and Standardization: it is the most essential unit of the suggested SHS. The data and information acquired by various sensors are generally in various formats. This information must be transformed into a single standard format  in order to  do efficient analytics. This unit also performs the duty of blockchain encryption of data and information. In this way, all the stakeholders in the system construct and sign intelligent contracts digitally. Since blockchain offers an unchangeable structure, it is seen as a tamper proof that it guarantees total security, consistency, and user privacy  (Tripathi et al., 2020) . I o T adoption in healthcare Healthcare is increasingly sensitive industry.  Regarding technology, both doctors and patients put a lot of emphasis on the privacy of personal data, diagnoses, and treatment. In addition, privacy and trust concerns are listed, healthcare providers' difficulties of technology implementation and ease of use. Therefore, technology implementation faces a lot of barriers from both customers' and providers' perspectives.    Privacy concern With the digitalization of industries worldwide, companies strive to provide professional and customized solutions to their customers. The same is true for healthcare systems, which increasingly collects and analyses patient's information to gain valuable insights. Even though big data results in improved services and is valued by many patients, some still raise concern about  the loss of privacy (Lanier & Saini, 2008). The main privacy concern appears because  other actors who have access to patients' private health information can use it inappropriately without the patient's knowledge and permission ( Chepalla  & Sin, 2005). Given the fast development of the Internet and further technological advancements the extent of public privacy awareness and concerns increases ( Dinev  & Hart, 2005). Several authors such as Bansal et al. (2010), Xu (2019) and  Dhagarra  et al (2020) examine privacy concerns and the reasons behind them in the healthcare industry. For instance,  Steininger  et al. (2015), in their study on the acceptance of Information Technologies in Healthcare, identified that privacy concerns harm the perception of HER or  electronic healthcare records.    Trust towards IT To assess the importance of trust in healthcare, peculiarities of the healthcare industry should be concerned . As healthcare is a sensitive topic, the trust issue is of special concern within the patients. In case healthcare provider cannot be trusted, the patient has no reason to use the services of this provider.  Academics define trust as a multiple and complex concept that includes several dimensions and is affected by numerous factors (Castaldo et al., 2010). Trust is defined as “a psychological state of accepting vulnerability towards positive expectations”. Trust is essential for building relationships and fruitful collaborative actions.  Bozic  (2017) claims that product acceptance, consumer loyalty and long-term relationships are based on trust.   Complexity  concerns  New information technology is not easy to understand for a regular person who never faced with it before. It is rather difficult to understand new  technologies, but  concerns on the ease of its potential use are  raised (Gao, Li, Luo, 2015).  Informed consent and physical safety Apart from the privacy and security concerns and trust  Ronaghi &  Mohammadi  (2020) in their study on ethical issues connected to the implementation of Internet of Things in healthcare sector list informed consent and physical safety. Interestingly, informed consent and privacy perceived by the consumers to be the most important ethical issues regarding IoT.  Given that IoT devices can, in theory, collect data of patients and healthcare consumers without their permission, such interrogation of IoT in private lives  is considered to be  harmful and encroaching upon patients' protection.  Perceived health threat In their study on wearables implementation in healthcare, Gao, Li, Luo (2015) argue that the health behaviours of consumers should be studied. The authors test 2 hypotheses concerning healthcare threat: “Perceived vulnerability is positively associated with individual’s intention to adopt healthcare wearable  devices; Perceived severity has positive influence on individual’s intention to adopt healthcare wearable devices.” While perceived vulnerability stands for the possibility of facing health-threat, perceived severity refers to the extent of the possible threat. Relevant studies approved the positive impact of health threat on the technology adoption. C hapter 2 .  Blockchain  in IoT and healthcare. 2.1. Blockchain adoption About two decades ago, the internet emerged as an unprecedented and highly disruptive technology that shook the foundations of many established businesses. This upheaval proliferated throughout many supply chains (SCs) and led to restructuring complete value networks (Yao et al., 2009;  Dresner  et al., 2001). While the use of network technology to exchange data electronically ( e.g.  EDIFACT) had existed long before, it was the widespread adoption of the internet for commerce (both B2B and B2C) that heavily impacted supply chains ( Lancioni  et al., 2003). The current blockchain development somewhat resembles those early days of internet adoption, which was slow at first. The roots of the ARPANET, which was an early packet switching network based on TCP/IP, date back to the end of the 1960s. However, it was not until the commercial potential of the internet was fully realized in the 1990s that the World Wide Web yielded many user-friendly applications with both positive and negative implications for businesses and individuals ( Kambil , 1995). With widespread adoption, the internet had a significant impact on supply chain management by creating electronic marketplaces, helping to realize cost reductions, increasing productivity, enabling e-procurement, integrating business  processes  and allowing for the creation of customized services ( Lancioni  et al., 2003). It is now the blockchain that offers similar promises, and a rigorous academic investigation is needed into the extent to which it may help businesses create value. The general pattern of interest in the blockchain exhibits several years of slow adoption followed by exponential growth starting at the end of 2015 and a spike of the end of 2017, caused by massive public interest in Bitcoin (Google Trends, 2018).  In the recent months of the end of 2020 and the beginning of 2021, the growth of blockchain due to the raised public interest could also be seen.  Like the internet, the blockchain is not a single technology. Still, it operates on a stack of technologies that includes the internet as an infrastructure and uses the blockchain protocol to store transaction records and establish consensus rules. Various applications such as smart contracts can be operated on the application layer, yet the full range of potential applications is still unclear (World Economic Forum, 2017). Proponents of the blockchain point out that it enables the creation of a so-called "Internet of Value" (Deloitte, 2016), through which property rights can be transferred online. This marks a key development from the “Internet of Information”, in which users can share information but cannot be prevented from creating infinitely many copies. The ongoing discussions regarding the potential impact of the blockchain and lack of research on this topic notwithstanding, many companies are investing huge sums of money in blockchain-based solutions that bear the potential to revolutionize  supply chains and services of numerous industries . According to Juniper Research, which conducted a study amongst 369 company founders, executives, managers, and IT experts, 76 per cent of respondents believe that the blockchain could be "very useful" or "quite useful" for their company, 35 per cent expect significant internal disruptions and 51 per cent anticipate significant disruptions to their partners or customers (Holden  &   Moar , 2017). An IBM study reports that one-third of organizations are considering or actively engaged with blockchain technology, with numbers varying by industry. Using input from 2,965 C-suite executives the IBM study reports that early adopters (“explorers”) are experiencing disruptions in their industry twice as often as so-called “passives” (60 vs 30 per cent) and that explorers are much more likely to disrupt a market or industry themselves by “changing the rules of the game” (21 vs 5 per cent) (IBM, 2017, p. 7). The whole blockchain market size is estimated to grow from US   $210.2m in 2016 to US   $2,312.5m by 2021, at a compound annual growth rate of 61.5 per cent (Markets and Markets, 2016). Even though blockchain can potentially create a world with a secure,  shared  and transparent for everyone databases where intermediaries are  rudimental  and all tasks are conducted frictionlessly by digital representation of individuals, organizations, or algorithms, this world will not appear soon.  Blockchain is characterized as not a "disruptive technology" that can overtake incumbent firms in a moment, but rather is considered a foundational technology, like the internet, that can develop whole social and economic systems. To achieve the blockchain revolution it needs to overcome technological, governance, organizational and societal barriers ( Iansiti  & Lakhani, 2017). Figure  2 . How foundational technologies take fold ( Iansiti  & Lakhani, 2017. A  Framework   for   Blockchain   Adoption The framework was developed  that mapped innovations  against two dimensions. The first is novelty, meaning the extent to which the technology is new to the world. Whereas the second dimension is complexity, describing the level of coordination in the ecosystem ( №  and diversity of parties) required to successfully produce value with the technology.  Single   use Low-novelty   and   low-coordination   applications   that   generate   better ,  less   expensive ,  highly   focused   solutions   are   found   in   the   first   quadrant .  For example, Bitcoin can be attributed to this quadrant. From the very beginning Bitcoin offered simple immediate value to those who used it as an alternative payment solution.  Localization The   second   quadrant   contains   inventions   that   are   very   innovative   yet   require   just  a  small   number   of   consumers   to   provide   immediate   benefit ,  making   their   adoption   relatively   simple .  Assuming   blockchain   makes similar journey like   network   technologies   have   taken   in   business ,  we   may   anticipate   blockchain   developments   to   be   based   on   single-use   apps   that   form   local   private   networks   to   which   many   businesses   are   linked   via  a  distributed   ledger .  This is already happening  in many financial firms, for example, JPMorgan, Bank of America,  Fidelity  and others have created a test version of blockchain to as a replacement for settlement in areas such as trade finance and securities.  Substitution The   third   quadrant   comprises   applications   with   low   novelty   since   they   expand   on   existing   single-use   and   localized   apps. However, the key difference is high demand for large scale  coordination   because   they   involve   wider   public   uses .  These   advancements   aim   to   substitute   entire   business   models .  The barriers are very high as not only the requirements for coordination are very high but also because third quadrant applications replace the processes that are deeply embedded within corporate entities and institutions.  Cryptocurrencies ,  which   are   new ,  fully   formed   currency   systems   that   grew   out   of   the   basic   bitcoin   payment   technology ,  are   examples   of   substitutes . Transformation In   the   last   quadrant   lie   totally   innovative   applications   which ,  if   successful ,  have   the   potential   to   transform   the   fundamental   nature   of   economic ,  social ,  and   political   institutions .  They   include   coordinating   the   activities   of   several   players   and   achieving   institutional   consensus   on   standards   and   processes .  Adoption   will   necessitate   significant   social ,  legal ,  and   political   changes . At   the   moment , “ smart   contracts ”  might   be   the   most   revolutionary   blockchain   use .  Smart contracts can   automate   exchange of value   and   the   transfer   of   money   or   other   assets   as   specified   criteria   are   satisfied . A  smart   contract ,  for   example ,  might   make  a  payment   to  a  supplier   in the same instant as  a  cargo   is   delivered  ( Iansiti  & Lakhani, 2017) .   2.2 . Description of blockchain technology To unders tand the blockchain technology,  it is important to look at the internet's foundations. The distributed architecture of the internet is based on a peer-to-peer network. In this P2P network, every computer is equal and can offer or request services from other computers. Blockchain technology can be seen as a large ledger based on a P2P network that records transactions which cannot be erased or changed. All network participants help manage the storage  of this information, and the network must verify a new record before it is added to the blockchain ( Xiong  et al., 2020).  Mistry et al (2019) describe blockchain as "a chain of time-stamped blocks that are linked together using cryptographic hashes". Meanwhile other researchers state that blockchain is "a database that stores all processed transactions (or data) in chronological order" ( Minoli ,  Occhiogrosso , 2019). Figure 3 . The scheme of Blockchain Process (Farouk et al, 2020). The key words in the Blockchain technology are “blocks” and “chain” or, in other words, chain of blocks.  The visual representation of the notion is shown in Figure 3.  When a person wants to make a transaction, the technology creates a unique block that includes all information on the transaction, specifically codified to include the part of a code of the previous block called "hash". Then, this codified transaction is broadcasted to every node involved in the transaction. In nodes, the verification of the block happens: if the block's code includes the right "hash", it is validated as an honest part of the chain according to the consensus algorithm. If not, the block and thus transaction is rejected. Key parts of which Blockchain  consists  are  from the business and technological perspective : Consensus, Ledger, Cryptography, Smart Contract. Consensus or   PoW  (Proof of Work) protocol is responsible for verification of each action in the network to prevent domination of the single node in the entire blockchain and, thus, mitigate possible manipulation of transaction history.  However, at this moment, new types of  Consensus  such as  PoS  (Proof of Stake) are developed.  Ledger is a shared database that stores information on all transactions in the network. Importantly, once the information is stored, it cannot be deleted at any means. Cryptography or codification makes sure that all data is secured with strong encryption and only authorized users can decrypt and understand information.  Martinez et al. (2020)  define cryptography as a way  of  transforming an unlimited string of characters to a fixed length.  Meanwhile ,  Smart contracts are used to validate the network participant (Mistry et al, 2019). Several authors argue that blockchain technology can be used in different industries such as agriculture and healthcare as a data storage and as an addition to the supply chain ( Xiong  et al., 2020). An important factor of blockchain technology is that all peers own the ledger in the network, making it impossible for one peer to corrupt it. Therefore, the digital asset is visible for everyone because of decentralization, and adjustments to the asset are recorded in real-time, which provides total transparency. Furthermore, b lockchain makes the history of any digital form of information immutable and transparent by using  multiple  peers in the network that help with the decentraliz ation and cryptographic hashing.    An interesting feature of blockchain introduced with Bitcoin is the Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) that is a part of  PoW . BFT means that two blocks of information can openly communicate and display the data, even if several blocks in the chain are not validated.    From the consumer perspective, it is easier to consider blockchain to consist of   transactions,  nodes, blocks, and miners (Martínez et al., 2020). The blocks contain data, a randomly generated number called the nonce, and a 256-bit hash number bound to the nonce ( Builtin , 2019). Miners create new blocks with a process called mining. During this process, Miners solve the mathematical problem of finding an accepted hash solution that connects with the last block, which will be added to the chain ( Builtin , 2019). The last aspect of the blockchain is the node. Nodes are the peers and owners of the ledger. Each peer in the network algorithmically approves any newly mined blocks by the miners to add this new data to the chain. Because of this high control intensity, it is possible to strictly verify the information and safety of the ledger ( Builtin , 2019). Eventually, each participating block in the chain is given a unique alphanumeric identification number, which helps to check and verify every activity in the chain. Furthermore, the safety of information that the ledger contains is of higher standards than a centralized database because of extreme difficulty in changing the chain. Changing a block requires not only re-mining of the block and its subsequent blocks. Therefore, this process demands even more computing power to find the needed math solution. For that reason, blockchain technology can deliver many new solutions for sharing and protecting generated data from people, companies, and governments.  Due to the fact that  blockchain is not a widely adopted technology, the complexity of understanding of its value and the difficulty of its usage by the general audience raise additional barriers for its implementation.  The majority of  consumers do not understand what they will need to do in case blockchain is implemented. For instance, in case of its implementation in healthcare  services  in order to  access the decrypted health information, the patient will need to enter the personal key to the blockchain network or grant this key to a  trustless  provider. Interestingly, the consumer will be in a full charge of his or her personal information, and when the key is lost, the access to information collected is lost as well. However, this works if we are considering the traditional blockchain and its initial values of decentralization. Meanwhile, the majority of appearing blockchains or blockchain-based technologies are  transformed and do not place decentralization control over information in the first place. 2.1.1. Key limitations of existing blockchain networks. A key limitation  of blockchain networks lie s  in its technological, governance and social barriers. The main challenge  of existing blockchain projects  is technological, which is represented by the blockchain trilemma . 2.1.1.1.  Technological barrier  – blockchain trilemma The transformed networks appear owing to the inability of traditional blockchain to solve the blockchain  trilemma .   Trilemma was invented by Ethereum founder  Vitalik   Buterin . Trilemma describes the trade - offs that cryptocurrency project creators  have to  make in order to optimize the structure of the blockchain. Simply put, you cannot do everything at once. There are three elements to the trilemma: decentralization, security, and scalability. Decentralization is the degree to which ownership, influence, and value are distributed across the blockchain. It is the degree of decentralization that  matters, since  it is not a binary component. Ethereum has a high degree of decentralization, Eos has a partial one, and Twitter does not have decentralization. Many people mistakenly believe that networks are decentralized or centralized, or that all blockchains are decentralized in the same way. In decentralized networks, the main control is in the hands of users, who, thanks to shares, can vote, use the services of the  platform  and make a profit. In all blockchains, users buy tokens for voting, but they receive different power of influence for this, depending on the structure of the network. In Proof of Ownership protocols, the strength of a user's vote depends on the number of tokens they hold. In delegated proof of stake protocols, participants use stakes to select a third party to vote on their behalf. However, most of this governance structure is coded, so voting is usually done to resolve disputes. In other systems, management usually decides to change strategy,  operations  and shareholder rights. The decentralized blockchain has another important property  that lies in the  fact that  most of the value belongs to the participants. There is no "management" - a centralized body that first takes its share and distributes the rest to the rest of the participants. Most cryptocurrency projects are not owned by founders, but by members or users  ( NeonVest , Viswanathan & Shah, 2018) . Among benefits power to society, transparency and safety of information can be listed. The rules of governance in the blockchain are  actually written  in the code and cannot be changed, so the network ensures the maximum distribution of power, wealth and ownership. Moreover, the more decentralized a system is, the more reliable it is - it does not have a central point of failure vulnerable to hacking. disadvantages Disadvantages include reduced efficiency in terms of speed and complications of elimination. In decentralized protocols such as Bitcoin and Ethereum, new blocks are created by miners which solve complex problems to find a hash. This takes a huge amount of energy, performance and speed are reduced, which negatively affects the efficiency of blockchains that require high bandwidth. A decentralized blockchain is difficult to shut down as it does not have a centralized server or authority. On the one hand, this is an advantage, but if the blockchain begins to cause damage and its participants cannot solve the problem, then the decentralized nature of the network will complicate the process of eliminating the blockchain  ( NeonVest , Viswanathan & Shah, 2018) . Security is the level of protection of the blockchain against external attacks, and from the point of view of the internal blockchain system - the degree of its immutability. Most blockchains are significantly exposed to security threats. There is a link between decentralization and security. Often, the more nodes a network has, the less it depends on a central authority, which means there is less risk of breaking a single point of failure. However, there are other types of attacks that threaten decentralized networks: > 50% attack occurs when a participant (or multiple participants)  receive  more than 50% of all tokens and take control of the entire network; Sibyl attack - a participant (or several participants)  create  many (hundreds, thousands and more) identifiers in the system in order to gain control over a large share of ownership and / or make decisions in the network; Spam attack - a participant (or several participants)  flood  the network with low-value transactions to stop the blockchain from working; A DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service Attack) attack occurs when an attacker disrupts network traffic by flooding it with malicious  transactions; Collusive attack - one or more participants (or nodes) agree to conduct malicious activity on the network. The major benefit of high security - protecting the blockchain from attacks. This is especially true for systems that require maximum security and work with confidential data.  As a downside secure network needs computational power and a high number of network participants which again slows down the efficiency  ( NeonVest , Viswanathan & Shah, 2018) . Scalability is an important component in determining network throughput. In other words, it sets an upper bound on the potential size of the network. The maximum number of users is the most important aspect of network evaluation. Today there are 2.9-5.8 million wallets on the Bitcoin network; Facebook has 4 billion  users,  Eos has several thousand. Scalability guarantees fast application performance and the ability to process a large volume of transactions, which is especially important for sites with audio / video streaming, games, and social networks.  In regard to  IoT, scalability is of crucial importance as IoT implies trillions of transactions. When more users are using the application than expected, the scaled network can handle the load. However, the main disadvantage of infinite scalability is the low level of security. Almost  all of  the security threats listed above are increasing on a large scale on the network. Actively growing networks must have a fast consensus mechanism  in order to  confirm more transactions and process user requests at the same speed. This is only possible in Proof of Ownership blockchains or Proof of Delegated Ownership. But in such a network, decentralization decreases. Proof-of-work protocols will have to simplify hash-finding tasks or mining algorithms to ensure fast transaction processing. This undermines security and to some extent, decentralization (mining pools will benefit if the tasks of finding the hash become easier). Providing infinite scalability requires some sacrifice. In a scalable and decentralized network, there is a high security threat. Developers choose the most suitable platform for their needs, and users choose the most productive. Some are willing to sacrifice security for scalability; others are willing to sacrifice scalability for security.  At the moment  there is no blockchain which would solve the trilemma, but lots of projects strive to solve it. Given that security and speed is of crucial importance for healthcare IoT, it should be a matter of further discussions to which levels decentralization is needed in the industry. 2.1.1.2.  Governance, organizational and social  barrier There is general lack of knowledge via the public on what blockchain is. Some people do not understand what digital currency mean and are far from understanding that value can be created from not material resources, but knowledge and design of the system. General lack of awareness and the beginning of blockchain adoption phase that includes a lot of free-riders and unethical participants in the network result in p ublic  scepticism, which, in turn, slows down its adoption. While individuals are not able to understand and use the technology, organizations are not able to trust the technology and implement it in operations as their employees are mostly not ready for the challenge. The perceived complexity of the technology which arises due to the lack of education on the topic also increases the adoption barriers. The fact that governments are slow in the regulation of the field or, in some cases, report that Bitcoin was  closed down  by government officials (which is impossible) approves the fact of the lack of public education. 2.1.2 .  Key benefits of blockchain  –  security ,  traceability  and transparency Blockchain could be a solution to  the number of problems such as  intraceability  and lack of private analytics as  the technology  is capable of storing  and sharing real-time information of certain products over  the whole  product  or service  lifecycle  in a secure manner  ( Xiong  et al., 2020).  All data points give a unique timestamp to the product that passes a station (a node) and make it immutable and transparent for all stakeholders involved in the supply chain (Kamath, 2018).   Blockchain is frequently discussed by academics in the context of supply chains  ( Kshetri , 2018; Brand, 2018; Queiroz &  Wambab , 2019; Wong et al., 2020 ).  Research state that blockchain brings numerous advantages to supply chain managers. Firstly, all transactions of either products or services become more transparent,  traceable  and efficient with blockchain implementation. Moreover ,  it is believed that blockchain can enhance the trust of the consumers towards the products as they can trace the whole journey of the item desired.  Accountability,  protection  and cybersecurity benefits are especially highlighted by  Kshetri  (2017).  Therefore, blockchain makes the supply chain system safer as it is rather hard to corrupt the transparent system without punishment from other actors. The traceability guarantees the fraud prevention making the supply chain operations safer and trustworthy. This, in turn,  is able to  enhance collaboration of nu merous actors in the system, increase system efficiency and reduce costs in the long run. However, even though multiple works are devoted to blockchain implementation in supply chain management the technology adoption is still at its infancy phase . Huge technological and supply chain corporations are taking small steps in the blockchain adoption.  For example, Walmart collaborated with IBM to use blockchain technology  in their supply chain . This food - to-table approach led to an improvement in tracing time from seven days to 2.2 seconds, which resulted in a high level of transparency across Walmart's supply chain (Kamath, 2018). The crucial factor that made this improvement possible is the use of the Hyperledger fabric. IBM used a blockchain solution based on this fabric  in order to  store and share billions of generated data points at the same time (Hyperledger White Paper Working Group, 2018).  The issue of scalability is widely discussed by blockchain researchers ( Minoli  &  Occiogrosso , 2018;  Builtin , Mistry et al.,  Makhdom  et al., 2019; Martinez et l., Farouk et al., 2020 ). IBM solved the problem of blockchain scalability  i n a creative manner.  Hyperledger fabric is developed to combine several blockchains and provide a modular architecture that enables a plug-and-play approach (Hyperledger White Paper Working Group, 2018). The Hyperledger makes it possible to capture and control data efficiently, which results in a shared view of the information that could be shared at any moment (Kamath, 2018). Eventually, IBM’s approach makes it possible to control different aspects of the supply chain, like agricultural treatments, manufacturers, and safety protocols, all available and stored in real-time (Kamath, 2018). Therefore, blockchain enables consumers  and  companies to map the supply chain and show all the critical factors that could be optimized to improve the quality and efficiency  of both products and services . The second implication of blockchain concerns the availab ility of real-time information  on products  and services , which gives the possibility to act on the misconduct of suppliers  or providers  leading to improved consumer trust ( Xiong  et al., 2020).   Using the traceability technology combined with blockchain storage and sharing capabilities will event ually lead to higher quality in global  supply chain s , including healthcare ones . Blockchain can use its characteristics to protect the healthcare products and services quality standards and give a clear overview of the global healthcare supply chain network. Two important implications are traceability and improved consumer trust.  However, these characteristics are partly  enable  by the decentralization characteristic of blockchain. 2.2. Blockchain and IoT Blockchain can potentially provide a set of benefits to IoT, which are data traceability, increased  security  and safety. However, both blockchain and IoT concepts have physical and technological limitations that barrier their further integration. 2.2.1 Synergy of IoT and Blockchain The blockchain was mainly linked to IoT because of the  security concerns. However, b lockchain technology  is able to  solve a number of existing IoT disadvantages connected to data storage, data sharing, data security and data circulation. Firstly, blockchain technology could track data exchanges between sensors/devices to other devices, gateways, or end-users. Secondly, automotive nature of blo ckchain  ass ure s  security and efficiency of  transactions that, in turn, leads to business efficiency  and cost-cuttings in long-term . Main advantages of the Blockchain implementation in IoT are: data security and privacy that turns into trust-building between business and customers; data integrity across a large number of transactional parties by provision of working proof of decentralized trust to all participants in the ecosystem; data storage capabilities by blockchain's ability to store information in an immutable manner, which requires no centralized database; long-term cost reductions and increased speed of transactions. Main challenges that the implementation of Blockchain in IoT faces are the lack of standardization and diversity of  International  laws on information security and electronic security. Blockchain has low scalability and compatibility.  Meanwhile, data storage capabilities for IoT devices can be questioned as well.   Each process should be independently empowered  and tailored to specific needs of IoT . Meanwhile existing IoT dev ices are incompatible with  PoW  due to the lack of computational power inside devices .  However, other consensus solutions such as proof of stake or proof of integrity  are able to  tackle the low computational power issue.   Other challenges that can be mentioned include high energy consumption by blockchain enabled solutions, lack of storage of IoT devices  which  is  ,  lack of user-friendly applications in the industry and lack of solid business cases with a decent history of implemented Blockchain in IoT. Overall, due to the ability of blockchain to provide decentralized control, it can modify and even make a revolution in  a number of  current and fu ture spheres. With the help of b lockchain,  the  consistency and privacy of numerous transactions and databases can be guaranteed. 2.2.2. Potential of blockchain Integration in IoT As could be seen from the review of the current projects, blockchain still strives to provide scalability, which becomes a crucial challenge in the  context of IoT.  At the moment  b lockchain poorly scales in situations with a large number of nodes which IoT network needs  ( Atlam  et al, 2018) . However,  the majority of  discussions of  the blockchain  inscalability  challenge are raised around the belief that all existing nodes in the network should store all historical transactions for the network to stay secure. Nevertheless, due to the increasing number of blocks in the network, it becomes harder and harder to participate in the validation of transaction in a popular blockchain network such as Ethereum. The scalability challenge is  now  addressed by the blockchain industry participant on several different layers.  2 scalability formats of blockchain networks – 1 layer -  sharding  (division of the network into smaller networks, 2 layer – optimistic rollups or  zk -SNARKS (the main network is secured, but parts of transaction volumes are made on the additional second layer).  On the one hand, the number of new Layer 1 blockchain project appear and build the ecosystem of decentralized applications around their network. On the other hand, the new algorithms, such as Merkle trees and  zk -SNARK algorithms are introduced in both Layer 1 and Layer 2 projects.  Zk -SNARK technology, for instance, is introduced by Mina Protocol blockchain which states to maintain the same size of the network regardless of the number of blocks produced.  Hedera  hashgraph  is a separate technology that tries to overcome scalability challenge. Solana  at the moment  can handle 50000 transactions per second. As scalability challenge is being addressed by the industry for several years already, it has a potential to be solved in the nearest time. However, there is a challenge connected to the speed of  transactions and its applicability to IoT. As IoT devices can produce millions of transactions per second, the validation mechanisms in blockchain network should be enormously fast. Moreover, even though the history of the whole blockchain network could stay of the same size, IoT devices or servers would still need an enormous computational power to ensure the efficiency of the network. Even though the year of 2020 and 2021 was the year where blockchain became trend and raised awareness of millions of people of its existence, the general awareness and understanding of the technology by the public remains limited. The same is true, but in a lower extent to IoT –  the majority of  people heard about the concept, but do not understand it. From a legal perspective, blockchain is perceived as a threat. From the one side, blockchain creates a threat for service providers and manufacturers  due to the fact that  blockchain is the  technology, software, and hardware, and one can create a node and use blockchain in the any part of the globe. From the other side it is rather hard to monitor the speed of blockchain development and create the regulatory environment for its control. Blockchain is new and widely unexplored territory (Pranav et al., 2021). Overall ,  the potential of blockchain integration in IoT is limited due to 1) issues that arise from blockchain challenges, and 2) issues that arise from IoT challenges. As for the blockchain challenges scalability  and speed , lack of skills, legal issues and lack of smart contracts are listed. Meanwhile, IoT challenges also include legal issues and lack of skills, but also a set of technological pitfalls such as small storage size, low computational  power  and low battery life  ( Reyana  et al, 2018) .  2.3.  Blockchain and healthcare While substantial research has been carried out in many application fields to explore the potential of blockchain, the healthcare field remains the one trailing behind. There are several causes for this. The key reason for this is the significant scarcity of medical blockchain expertise (Tripathi et al., 2020). However, there are also a variety of challenges for the blockchain adoption in healthcare domain such as:  In the present SHS operations, there is a lack of standard data collecting, sharing, and interoperability tools. Smart contracts are not viable when patients are unable to provide access to their private keys/ personal medical data (for example, the patient might be unconscious).  Data transfer from EMR (electronic medical records) to blockchains is complicated  Lack of complete absence of standard government policies and protocols establishing the norms and regulations of fair blockchain technology usage encompassing all elements such as data ownership, legal concerns,  kinds  and nature of sanctions in case of  misbehavior , and so on. The implementation of blockchains can make the entire healthcare system data- centric  ( data from EMR, wearable devices, etc.); nevertheless, this is not acceptable at a certain point because human interaction is required to better comprehend a patient's condition. The disruptive features primarily lie in blockchain’s ability to create trust and security  which are specifically valued in the healthcare industry due to high data sensitivity. Apart from the traceability characteristics and its benefits blockchain can propose  the ability to integrate. In fact, blockchain systems may replace entire supply chain record - or tracking systems due to its immutability, quickness, and trustworthy  characteristics (Brand, 2018).  For example,  in regards to  the food supply chain, blockchain's traceability characteristic prevents situations where entire batches of food are thrown away in the case of one batch of contaminated food (Kamath, 2018). If applied to healthcare industry blockchain can replace all healthcare records and store each change within the records promoting security through transparency and cryptography. At the moment  the customers' privacy concerns that the healthcare industry is exposed to results in challenges connected to the analysis of data. Therefore, big data solutions cannot be applied to guarantee increased product and service quality and efficiency. However, blockchain can enable the safe encrypted storage of such data, access to which can be granted with a specific key to a limited number of healthcare actors. In case the data from healthcare records would be encrypted and available only to specific actors with a granted permission from the patient, the anonymous data analysis would be enabled. For instance ,  blockchain can encrypt the owners of specific medical healthcare record and share this data in an encrypted form with other medical actors in a secure manner.  The major advantages of blockchain implementation in healthcare are  security of data and prevention of data corruption owing to smart contracts and system immutability, guaranteed by decentralization, and anonymity and privacy enabled by cryptography. Meanwhile, the main difficulty in the implementation of blockchain technology in healthcare lies in the infant stage of its public adoption and the complexity of the blockchain understanding which raises public scepticism.  Moreover, other challenges include the complexity of controlling your personal medical data via blockchain and the lack of established regulatory frameworks.  In particular cases , for instance, when a person is in a coma or in other circumstances that limit their cognitive and physical ability to remember and share keys to the medical data stored via blockchain, the data will be lost and could not be used for enhanced medical treatment.  Even in less extreme circumstances, the person can either forget or lose the keys opening its blockchain information. Therefore, i n the case of healthcare, the responsibility over the security and access to the data fully lies on the person owning the data.   C hapter  3. Potential of blockchain integration in IoT devices of  Healthcare  industry 3.1. Prerequisites of blockchain integration in healthcare IoT The combination of blockchain and IoT may offer additional positive impact points for the healthcare industry. Blockchain serves as the track and trace tool, in which blocks of secure, non-mutable data is stored. IoT systems, in this respect, serve as the data tools. With the integration of blockchain and IoT in the food industry, farmers may be insured for bad weather conditions or bacteria outbreaks, and it is often very difficult to prove such instances. With blockchain and automatic measurements via IoT, farmers are backed up securely (Brand, 2018). The same is applicable for healthcare, where doctors can be warned by smart technologies, IoT devices about healthcare threats of a particular person. The transparent data received from IoT devices and stored by the blockchain can serve as an insurance for both doctors and patients.  Complementing insurance, blockchain’s security and traceability characteristics enable additional applications raising from inter and cross-industry collaborations. For instance, in agricultural supply chains, blockchain and IoT enable the creation of the range of applications such as  AgUnity  which helps farmers increase income 2x-3x ( Picco , 2019) and secure payments, and  Agridigital , which offers smart contracts between buyers and sellers, that  take into account  the transparent, secure information that blockchain offers.  In regard to  blockchain and IoT in healthcare, the transparency of secure data it provides may leverage health data analytics applications. However, having multiple challenges from both IoT and blockchain business-side  in terms of the technology design , it is of especial interest how consumer perceive these technologies and its implementation to healthcare services.  In order to  understand it, the paper looks at the technology acceptance models w h ich are frequently used by the researchers to assess the perception of consumers towards modern technologies. 3.2. Technology acceptance models The concepts of blockchain and IoT are not yet widely known, therefore when considering their potential of implementation in healthcare or any industry, it is important to look at technology acceptance models. 3.2.1. TAM As the paper is devoted to the integration of technologies to humans' life, it is essential to discuss the theories of ICT acceptance or Technology Acceptance Models (TAM). Technology Acceptance Model was proposed by Davis (1989) and is aimed at understanding of behaviour of individuals towards ICT adoption. Even though his model was simplistic and included only 3 factors, which are perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use, his model created a foundation for future models (Brown et al., 2010;  Maruping  et al., 2017).  Dhagarra  et al. (2020) use the TAM model with two additional latent behavioural constructs essential for Information Technology adoption in healthcare, which are trust and privacy concern. Shrestha &  Vassileva   (2019)  use the TAM model and extend  it to include 4  dimensions  of the constructs  which are :   perceived usability, quality of the system, perceived  enjoyment  and perceived ease of use. Through this 4 both behavioral and quality constructs, the study explores how 4 dimensions affect the intention to use blockchain-based systems.  TAM framework  (see Figure  4 ) was  also exploited by  Jain et al.  (2020) to assess blockchain acceptance in logistics industry. Figure  4 . TAM to  assess blockchain acceptance in logistics indus try (Jain et al., 2020). The paper by   Karamchandani  et al .  (20 20 ) proposes the framework that unites  3  dimensions, technological (blockchain) ,  supply management constructs  and moderators, and uses the TAM model to assess the benefits of blockchain implementation to supply management systems  (see Figure  5 ). Figure  5 .  R esearch model on Perceived usefulness of blockchain implementation in supply management systems ( Karamchandani  et al., 2020). TAM and extended TAM is used to assess public perceptions on the technology in different industries. Questions to operationalize the constructs of TAM are tailored to the specific needs of the industry and technology characteristics. 3.2.2. UTAUT Venkatesh et al. (200 2) systemized the previously proposed models of acceptance into the UTAUT or Unified Theory of Acceptance and Use of Technology in his paper on customer acceptance of new technology. UTAUT model consists of performance expectancy, social influence, effort expectancy, and  a number of  facilitating conditions to predict personal intention for use of specific technology.   Performance expectancy is characterized as the degree to which an acceptance of an  innovational  technology will bring effectiveness to clients in the performance of certain activities. Within the setting of healthcare wearable gadgets, the performance expectancy can be respected as the degree to which the gadget can  offer assistance  buyers to screen every day physical conditions, make individual healthcare plans, and decrease health-related danger, etc. This term of seen anticipation moreover can be treated as reaction viability in PMT hypothesis (Sun et al., 2013) and seen advantage in security calculus hypothesis (Sharma and  Crossler , 2014). When customers can suggest that using healthcare wearable devices can empower them to analyse and maintain their health, they are more likely to accept the  innovation (Gao, Li & Luo, 2015) . In his  further  work on customer acceptance, Venkatesh et al.  (2012)  also proposes such constructs as hedonic motivation, pr ice value and habit to be used.  Overall, he creates UTAUT2 which includes 7 constructs among which are effort and performance expectancy, facilitating conditions, price value, social influence, hedonic motivation, and habit. As UTAUT has been used in several research papers assessing the utilization of cloud services (Wang, 2017), apps for social networking and different technological systems ( Batara  &  Nurmandi , 2017), and even for evaluation of blockchain accepta nce (Queiroz  &  Wambab , 2019). The research on blockchain integration into supply systems by  Queiroz and  Wambab  (2019) uses UTAUT models and expands it with 2 new constructs which are blockchain transparency, and trust of supply chain stakeholders. The figure explaining all concepts is presented  in Appendix  1 .   Gao, Li & Luo (2015) examine the acceptance of blockchain  and wearables integration in healthcare through UTAUT2 model by Venkatesh (2012). As can be seen, both TAM and UTAUT models are used to assess the consumer adoption of blockchain and IoT in different industries. Therefore, these models are  useful for the evaluation of acceptance of blockchain based IoT in Healthcare from the consumer perspective. 3.2.3. The technological dimensions Both blockchain and IoT are advanced technological concepts t h at are not easy to grasp and understand.  Therefore, when considering potential implementation such technological concepts as Relative advantage and Complexity of the technology are essential to understand the potential (Wong et al., 2020). In case the organization or consumer has not the infrastructure and resources needed for the implementation of new  technology,  it will not consider its adoption without a clear understanding of potential gains. Interestingly, the adoption of blockchain argued to bring intangible and, thus, frequently overseen benefits, such as increased customer loyalty, trust, and satisfaction. Relative advantage means the positive difference between gains and costs of implementation of new technology. This concept is seen as essential in understanding the technology adoption by Kapoor et al. (2014) and Wamba et al. (2018). Similarly, Bhattacharya & Wamba (2015) define complexity as both the complexity of technology and its implementation. It is worth mentioning that at this moment, blockchain implementation seems to be rather complicated for non-tech user. Yet to mention that a low percentage of the global population understands the  concept of blockchain which seems to be  challenging.  Moreover, it imposes additional complexity when multiple parties’ collaboration is applied. Users are believed to be confused and frightened by the complexity of technology. As the complexity of technology shapes the individual perception of this technology, complexity is negatively correlated with the possibility of technology adoption ( Alalwan , Dwivedi, & Rana, 2017). 3.2.4. Environmental dimensions Wong et al. (2020) lists environmental dimensions, which are marker dynamics, regulatory support, and competitive pressure among critical in the adoption of technology and blockchain  technology in particular .  In regard to  the healthcare industry, the regulatory support and market dynamics dimension are essential as the healthcare industry is highly monitored and controlled by the government and international institutions. Market dynamics dimension represents the changes in the environmental context around  particular technology . Market dynamics imply the technology adoption phase and competitive pressures from actors in the supply chains, industry standards and business models (Shi & Yan, 2016). Meanwhile, regulatory support stands for the extent to which the technology receives support from the governmental entities. Positive regulatory environment is listed among the necessary factors pertaining blockchain adoption. However,  at the moment , both indus try standards, actors and regulat ory environment are  not developed to favour the implementation of decentralized technology such as blockchain (Guo and Liang, 2016). Understanding of healthcare-relevant,  technological  and environmental factors influencing the blockchain and IoT in healthcare are relevant for the assessment of blockchain-enabled IoT potential from the consumer and business perspective. 3.3. Farouk et al (2020) and  Minoli  &  Occhiogrosso  (2019) concentrate on blockchain characteristics such as are fault tolerance, cryptographic security, and decentralized architecture. What is interesting is that  the majority of   the research  papers on the cross-section of IoT, blockchain, and healthcare are conference materials , which discuss the topic from the theoretical point-view, rather than practical  ( Rathee  et al., Pradhan et al., 2020; Arul & Renuka, 2021 ).  While  Arule  & Renuka (2021) present the summary  of existing blockchain protocols relevant for IoT,  Rathee  et al.
