Such blockchain solution is possible due to the low number of patients examined and low throughput. Research literature is rather limited in providing explanation on how blockchain-based IoT solutions can be accepted  at the moment   by  both business and consumers in the h ealthcare industry. Overall, previous  researches  on the topic have a number of demerits.  Lots of studies examine the acceptance of Information technologies in healthcare from efficiency, quality and cost-effectiveness  view-points   of  business perspective. Meanwhile  feasibility of proposed solutions,  behavioural constructs and consumer perspective are overlooked. Studies on  the pa tient's acceptance of IT, especially on the not  so  widely known IT advancements such as blockchain and IoT in healthcare, are lacking.  While articles frequently examine the challenges and advantages of blockchain implementation into IoT devices from the theoretical perspective or based on case studies, they rarely talk about consumer perspective and do not discuss the current state of business on the cross-section of three term s  – blockchain, IoT and healthcare.  METHODOLOGY The goal of the paper is to provide an overview of the potential of blockchain implementation in IoT in healthcare industry. The paper has 2 main research  sub-goals : To assess the potential of blockchain-enabled IoT devices in healthcare  from  business  perspective; To assess the perception of blockchain enabled IoT devices in healthcare from a consumer perspective. Due to the specificity of the research goal, the design of the paper is both descriptive and exploratory.  In regard to  the first question, the research describes the current state of blockchain, IoT and healthcare from the technological and business perspective. Moreover, it describes the conditions essential for I o T in healthcare from a consumer perspective.  However, the paper also examines current blockchain projects developed for IoT and healthcare as well as explores how consumers perceive blockchain and IoT implementation in healthcare services. The paper uses a mixed-method approach throughout the research. Firstly, it uses such qualitative method as thematic analysis of the literature to describe the current state of IoT, blockchain, and healthcare and compare existing business propositions. Secondly, the paper uses a qua ntita tive method to assess the consumers’ perception of blockchain and IoT  in regard to  healthcare. In order to  reach the first research goal, the thematic analysis of existing articles  which include  the following key words is conducted: IoT, blockchain, integration of IoT in blockchain, and healthcare.   The articles examined are the articles from the International Scientific journals on Management, Business, Entrepreneurship and Technology and Conference proceedings retrieved from Scopus,  ScienceDirect, Springer,  Elsevier, and  Ebsco  databases. The thematic analysis is conducted to understand the existing advantages and challenges of blockchain and IoT  in regard to  their cross-integration in the healthcare industry. Moreover,  in order to  extend the understanding of the current potential of implementation of blockchain-enabled IoT devices in healthcare, existing businesses that develop blockchain ne t works for IoT are compared and discussed.  The data for the  content analysis  is retrieved from the public online sources,  CrunchB ase , IBM and  Consensys  blog as well as  Coinmarketcap  aggregator of prices of blockchain currencies  and  crypto   projects .  The second research question is  solved by a quantitative method.  Based on the search of technology acceptance models in sphere of IoT, blockchain and healthcare , the Table  2  below was created. All survey questions will be based on the  5 -point Likert scale  measuring each item with 1 being “strongly disagree” and  5  being “strongly agree”  to enable  further statistical comparison and profound analysis.  The same methodology was used by Gao, Li, Luo (2015) in the assessment of wearables adoption in healthcare .   In order to analyse the data and make conclusions on the set of  hypothesis  described above, structural equation modelling (SEM) will be applied.  The survey sample consists of  252  people from different countries .  The majority of  respondents are from Russia, however the list of countries participating in the survey includes Kazakhstan, The Netherlands, UK, France, Germany, Portugal, China, Moldova, Algeria, India, Sudan, Qatar, Belarus, Ukraine and Israel.  The respondents surveyed represent different age and gender groups and have different levels of health, blockchain and IoT knowledge. After cleaning and editing the data of 200 respondents' results are chosen for the final analysis. The next step is the description of the data in terms of the minimum and maximum values, means, and standard deviations. Furthermore, the latent constructs are checked for reliability and validity through the calculation of Cronbach's Alpha, Exploratory Factor Analysis (EFA), assessment of the goodness of fit in terms of AVE and CR by Confirmatory Factor Analysis (CFA) and checking the constructs for discriminant validity based on  Fornell-Larcker's  criterion. The analysis includes multiple modifications of the proposed models to assure  good  reliability,  validity  and  model fit. The final step of the analysis is the testing of the full model effects and formulation of the results of hypotheses-testing. Consumer perspective The paper is aimed to test the perceptions of modern audiences towards IoT implementation in healthcare.  In order to  test a set of hypotheses the clean survey data of 200 respondents is used. The research of consumers inspects 11 latent independent constructs which are: Performance expectancy (PEXP), Facilitating conditions (FCON), Complexity of IoT use (CLX), Privacy concerns (PCON), Perceived privacy risk (PPR), Hedonic motivation of IoT use (HM), Patient-clinician communication (PCC), Willingness to share (WS). Complexity of blockchain use (CLXB), Self-efficacy (SE) and Hedonic motivation from blockchain use (HMB), and 3 dependent constructs – Use Intention of IoT (UIIOT), Blockchain (UIB) and Blockchain-enabled IoT (UI). Moreover,  DISCUSSION With regards to the waves of disruption  in its broad notion  (Teixeira, 2014), blockchain in healthcare may provide the industry with opportunities to  dis intermediate  and  decouple the consumer’s value chain. Disintermediation means that links in the supply chain are broken down by going more directly to the consumers. Traditionally, the supply chain of products, including healthcare products, involved many middlemen (Brand, 2018). Blockchain may support the industry by disintermediating, due to the situation with full information on healthcare products , enabled by blockchain transparency,  and services where middlemen are cut out (Brand, 2018). Furthermore, blockchain ’s ability to  information  storing  in a transparent manner, even for consumers, essentially  will  decoupl e  the non-value creating portion of informing and evaluating  done by healthcare actors  (Teixeira, 2014). Since consumers are more aware of what they buy or receive from healthcare providers, they value having complete information about the source of their medicine and particular recommendations in the medical prescription (Hancock, 2017).  From the theoretical perspective t he effects of both blockchain and IoT in the  healthcare  industry are in a lot of aspects transformative and disruptive for the traditional way of business. The most impactful will these two technologies be when they complement one another, resulting in complete, transparent, data-driven information along the entire medical supply chain. These technologies could essentially be complemented by technologies such as AI  to further increase the quality of healthcare products and services. It is believed that medical industry will be forced to go through a radical digital transformation in the next 30 years due to increasing socio-economic pressures from global population growth - growing from 7.8 billion today to an expected 9.7 billion in 2050 (Beer, 2020). This transformation will be driven out of necessity as current medical providers’ services and technologies are not efficient enough. Meanwhile, consumers in richer countries are demanding more transparency about their product and services choices (Xia et al., 2018). These trends and existing challenges present enormous opportunities for technologies such as IoT and blockchain in the  healt hcare industry.  Moreover, t heir integration of both these technologies can potentially revolutionize the healthcare practices.  However, as shown by the analysis of existing companies engaged in the development of blockchain-enabled IoT, at this moment there is no feasible solution that would create blockchain-enabled IoT to suit the needs of healthcare in privacy,  transparency  and safety. This happens due to the inherent technological challenges of blockchain (blockchain trilemma of scalability ,  decentralization  and security)  and IoT (low memory space and computational power, high exposal to safety threats, including physical) that are still to be solved. Blockchain technology ,  in particular ,  has  a lot of potential within the healthcare industry given its privacy and transparency issues.  The same is proved by the analysis that showed the increasing number of blockchain developers of healthcare and wellness broad and niche applications.  However, due to the  still low current adoption rate of the blockchain and the  high complexity  of its  implementation,  especially from the legal perspective,  it will only see limited application by the developed market leaders in the short term ( i.e.  Walmart case).  A lthough the market for blockchain technology is expected to grow to $429.7 million by 2023, it only represents a small percentage of the IoT market (Research and Markets, 2019). Nevertheless, the explosive growth in investment and market size of both technologies is a clear indicator that the  world  is increasingly vulnerable to digitization  and technologies will further grow .   However, it is important to note that blockchain may be not the best solution that is able to resolve privacy concern in healthcare. Authors exploring IoT discuss specific developments of IoT such as BAN or Anonymous Schemes.  BAN stands for Secure  and Lightweight Body Area Network )  which aimed to solve the privacy issue through implementation of authentication mechanisms  enabling  user  untraceability  and data secrecy  (Deng et al., 2017) .   Hongtao  et al (2018) create anonymous schemes for ensure privacy-preserving data collection in healthcare sector. Others use solutions enabled by decentralization of blockchain solely such as  PrivacyProtector  discussed by Luo et al. (2018) or encryption-enabled solutions discussed by Khan et al. (2020). The analysis of the current market of IoT in healthcare also identified two companies  ( Cynerio ,  Cylera )  aimed at the improvement of IoT cybersecurity for healthcare. From the consumer perspective it was identified that the audiences are willing to use IoT in healthcare services, however they are not sure about the use of blockchain and blockchain-enabled IoT. While 55% of respondents answer “agree” or “strongly agree” with the statement “ I would like to use IoT to manage my personal healthcare information ”, 20% identify that they are not sure. This can be due to the low awareness on the concept of IoT in general – 70% of respondents either “never heard about IoT” or “heard about it several times”.   Meanwhile 64% of respondents identify that they either “agree” or “strongly agree” with the statement “ I want to use IoT healthcare solutions in the future ”. Therefore, it can be concluded that the audiences are mostly positively perceive the introduction of IoT in healthcare. However, when sharing their attitude towards the use of blockchain in healthcare or blockchain-enable IoT in healthcare the majority of respondents  answer  “not sure”, which can be again an indicator of low knowledge on the concept. It was identified that performance expectancy has a high positive effect on the consumers’ intention to use IoT. Consumers value the opportunities to receive the enhanced transparent communication between patients and health providers, to increase control over their health seeking and monitoring and to increase overall healthcare efficiency. The research approves the consumers demand towards developed healthcare services. Moreover, as has been hypothesized consumers have concerns about the privacy of their healthcare data and are afraid of the inappropriate activities with their data both from healthcare and IoT providers. Interesting results of the analysis  are connected with  blockchain. Firstly, Privacy construct proved  to  has lower negative effect on the blockchain-enabled IoT. Secondly, Self-efficacy, the extent to which consumers can understand how to use blockchain on their own, positively affects the intention to use blockchain-enabled IoT. Finally, there is an unexpected result showing that the complexity of blockchain positively affects the intention to use blockchain-enabled IoT. This may indicate that the solution of blockchain-enabled IoT should be presented as a complex technology by nature, but simple technology to understand and use.  CONCLUSION The paper is devoted to the identification of the potential of blockchain technology implementation in healthcare IoT from business and consumer perspective.  Therefore  the paper analyses the research fields of IoT and blockchain in healthcare in regards to two research questions: 1) what is the potential (feasibility) of  current blockchain business  solutions for its integration in healthcare IoT? and 2) what is the level of consumers’ acceptance of IoT and blockchain-based IoT for healthcare? In order to  reach the research goal and to identify the potential of blockchain-based IoT in healthcare the paper summarizes the theoretical knowledge on the key benefits and challenges of both blockchain and IoT that can serve as barriers for their integration. Furthermore, the paper presents the summary of the current projects working in the field of IoT in healthcare and blockchain in healthcare by analysing projects from CrunchBase,  Coinmarketcap  and scientific articles reviewed. And finally, the paper conducts the survey of consumers to identify their perceptions on IoT and blockchain in healthcare. Theoretical and practical contribution From theoretical perspective the potential of the blockchain-enabled IoT in healthcare is enormous  as it  is able to  enable  transparent, data-driven information  and decisions  along the entire medical supply chain  – drug testing, quality of healthcare services, medical insurance, and consumers’ health control and monitoring. However, both these technologies are at their infancy stages of adoption and lack legal framework that would favour their development. Moreover, both IoT and blockchain have massive technological challenges that are yet to be solved. The paper presents the following list of conclusions on the business perspective potential of blockchain-enabled IoT in healthcare: There  are discrepancies  in regard to  current blockchain feasibility towards IoT development and its theoretical potential. Blockchain trilemma,  inscalability  of the majority of decentralized and secure blockchain networks, and IoT challenges of low security, low computational  power  and low memory, are the major barriers for blockchain integration with healthcare IoT which implies millions of secure transactions in low time frame.  As explained in earlier sections the transaction methods of blockchain involve a crucial speed concern. In other words, current blockchains are nowhere near the required speed for use in IoT. Furthermore, its adoption would be hampered in part by its immature  security issues.   Users will get concerned if they have little influence over the system's result, and businesses will be less inclined to adopt this new technology if it is complicated and incompatible with present  procedures; No companies  at the moment  creat e  blockchain-enabled IoT for healthcare –  there are  only individual experiment al  research for niche medical field with low number of data transactio ns ( Pradhan et al., 2020 ); 8 companies provid e  blockchain-solutions for healthcare  and  13 companies, mostly from US and Japan, develop IoT for healthcare industry.   The majority of  the companies are 2-3 years old and at the seed phases , which means that the field of smart healthcare has just started to develop. From consumer perspective the four following hypotheses are approved: P erformance expectancy has a high positive effect on the consumers’ intention to use  IoT; Patient clinician communication  is  positively  associated with the I ntention to  use ; Risk dimension (Perceived privacy risk and Privacy concern)  is  negatively  associated with  the  I ntention to  use ; Perceived Self-efficacy  of blockchain understanding is  positively  associated with the I ntention to use . The results presented imply that it is important to show that IoT can enhance consumers’ control over their health monitoring and seeking as well as enhance overall communication with healthcare providers and their productivity. Moreover, healthcare actors or IoT vendors that implement IoT should create mechanisms to prove the consumers’ that their health data is private and cannot be corrupted by any actor, including them. Blockchain can serve as one of such mechanisms as the effect of Privacy construct on blockchain-enabled IoT proved to be lower than on traditional IoT.   While it is generally believed that t he technical intricacy of blockchain is hard for people to grasp and trust , thus, it may hinder   desire to participate, the research proved the opposite. The complexity of blockchain is positively correlated with the intention to use it. Moreover, consumers show the self-efficacy in learning and understanding blockchain concept which is positively associated with the intention to use blockchain-enabled IoT. However, technical intricacies of blockchain adoption can be true  in regard to  business users and clinics representatives. Unless  blockchain technology can easily be integrated into current systems , the users will be reluctant to use it .  Overall, the paper adds to the scientific knowledge the summary of blockchain and IoT potential. Moreover, it extends the scientific knowledge on the consumers’ acceptance of such  technologies as IoT, blockchain and blockchain-enable IoT in healthcare. From the perceptive of practical implications of the research, the paper summarizes the current state of blockchain and IoT healthcare market with the lack of feasible business solutions, which can be useful for healthcare providers, government actors   regulati ng  blockchain and IoT ,  and business representatives that are willing to develop  IoT  technologies for healthcare sector. The consumer perspective of the research can be of use for the  above mentioned  actors to concentrate on the key features of IoT needed for consumers. Furthermore, the created technology acceptance model may serve as a foundation for further research works on the topic. Limitations   and future research directions The major limitations of the research are based around time-constraints and unavailability of practical (case-study) data on the topic due to the infant adoption stage of both IoT and blockchain in healthcare industry. Moreover, the research does not dwell into technological characteristics of blockchain and IoT and does not discuss the energy concerns raised both by blockchain and IoT use (Feng et al., 2020). From the business perspective the limitations include the lack of publicly available data on private blockchain development advancements of huge technological giants, such as ADEPT development by IBM and the lack of reliable sources that would list feasible blockchain and IoT projects.  The paper uses CrunchBase and  Coinmarketcap  databases to identify current blockchain and IoT projects in healthcare, however, the list of sources can be significantly increased in further research.  From the consumer perspective the research mostly examines Russian population (70% of all respondents), therefore the sample for model testing can include more international representatives. Furthermore, SEM analysis was neither able to approve nor disapprove a set of hypotheses on Facilitating conditions, Hedonic Motivation and Complexity of IoT effect on the consumers’ intention to use due to the limitations of the collected data. This means that future research paper can re-test the hypotheses stated in the paper and add new constructs to increase the fit of the final models. Furthermore, the research briefly mentions a set of possible moderators for the constructs’ effect on the use of IoT in healthcare, but their moderation effects are not extensively tested. Therefore, there is a space for moderations effect testing as one of the further research directions. REFERENCES Alalwan , A.A., Dwivedi, Y.K., Rana, N.P. (2017).
